<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: virtual-context-abi
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/virtual-context-abi.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/virtual-context-abi.qmd@static-context-abi-merge
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# The Virtual Context ABI: Typed Pages, Cells, and Certificates

A model can sound as if it remembers everything. A governed system has to be
able to say what it actually gave the model.

The Virtual Context ABI is the interface between durable memory and the
working context a model is allowed to see. It says what can be addressed, which
version is being mounted, which snapshot is current, which representation is
being used, what authority labels travel with the packet, how long the packet
is leased, and what kind of fault must be reported if the context is missing,
stale, unsafe, conflicting, or inadequate.

That is only half of the problem. Once context enters the working surface, the
system still needs to know what each piece is allowed to mean. An exact source
excerpt, a lossy planning summary, a redacted execution instruction, a claim
cell, a contradiction pair, and a reader paraphrase may all be made of words,
but they are not the same object. They do not carry the same authority or the
same evidential weight.

In reader terms, context becomes governed memory only when it has an identity,
a certificate, a lease, an authority boundary, and a fault path.

The local contribution is addressability with consequences. The live book uses
records, gates, receipts, and residuals everywhere; the ABI applies that
pattern to the moment memory becomes working context. A retrieved passage is
not enough. The system needs to know which version was mounted, what was lost,
who may use it, what certificate travels with it, and what fault should appear
when the packet is stale, unsafe, missing, or inadequate.

This follows directly from semantic IR. If the compiler gives obligations
addresses, the context system has to give evidence and memory addresses. A
semantic atom cannot honestly preserve a source, constraint, or policy if the
context behind it arrives as anonymous prompt material.

## Problem

Long-horizon agents need a stable context interface whose addresses, versions,
materializations, typed pages, context cells, certificates, authority labels,
loss contracts, adequacy states, and faults remain inspectable across
planning, reasoning, execution, and audit.

Without that interface, memory becomes prose. A retrieved passage, old
conversation, compressed summary, redacted brief, policy excerpt, source
quote, and derived inference can all arrive as plain tokens. The model can use
them, but the stack cannot later explain which object was supplied, which
version it came from, what was omitted, which authority ceiling applied, which
uses were permitted, whether the packet was adequate for the target claim, or
which fault should have blocked use.

VCM is therefore framed as an ABI: a stable calling convention for context. A
planner should be able to request a context packet by semantic address,
version, mount, snapshot, representation need, authority label, adequacy
target, and lease boundary. The memory system should respond with a
materialization receipt or a typed fault, not a best-effort blob.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Long context windows, retrieval systems, and summaries can move text close to a
model without defining stable addressability, representation authority, source
bindings, omission records, permitted uses, adequacy boundaries, or typed
failure behavior.

A larger window gives the model more room. It does not tell the system whether
a passage is current, authorized, adequate for verification, safe for the
worker, or traceable to a source. Retrieval answers "what looks relevant?"
The ABI has to answer harder questions: which version, under which mount, for
which task, under which authority, with which representation losses, and with
what failure behavior?

Summaries add another failure mode. A summary may preserve the main point
while dropping dissent, chronology, uncertainty, exact wording, source
revocation, or a limit on who may use it. That kind of loss is easy to miss
because the output still reads fluently. Semantic pages and certificates do
not make summaries automatically true. They make the loss, provenance,
authority, permitted use, and revocation state visible before reuse.

The ABI is therefore a discipline of role preservation. A useful summary may
orient planning without supporting quotation. A redacted packet may enable a
tool without exposing the source. A reader paraphrase may clarify an idea
without becoming evidence. The interface exists so those roles do not collapse
just because they share a context window.

## Core Claim

Virtual Context Memory should expose a Virtual Context ABI that materializes
typed pages and context cells with stable addresses, versions, certificates,
authority ceilings, loss/use contracts, adequacy states, residuals, and typed
faults. This remains an architectural argument.

The live chapter stays at `argument` support. The repository has source notes,
schema validation, finite Lean hooks, a synthetic context admission/adequacy
harness, and a deterministic VCM resolver/certificate probe over generated
public-safe source and derived cells. It does not have a deployed VCM
resolver, planner-guided context compiler, open-domain summary-fidelity
evaluator, certificate truthfulness checker, deletion-closure implementation,
transaction-isolation test, model-facing context run, VCM-Bench reproduction,
leak-prevention benchmark, or MoECOT runtime trace.

## Mechanism

The ABI turns a memory request into either a governed materialization or a
typed refusal.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Context request"] --> B["Address, version, mount, snapshot"]
  B --> C["Typed page or context cell"]
  C --> D["Representation certificate"]
  D --> E{"Use allowed?"}
  E -- "yes" --> F["Materialized context packet"]
  E -- "no" --> G["Typed fault or residual"]
  F --> H["Plan, claim ledger, artifact, or reader use"]
  G --> H
```

The diagram is not a resolver implementation. It shows the contract. A context
request must pass through address, version, mount, snapshot, representation,
certificate, authority, admission, and adequacy before it becomes usable
model-visible context. Failure is not an exception to hide. It is one of the
ABI's official outputs.

The mechanism separates four states that ordinary retrieval often collapses.
A request can be well formed. A reference can resolve. A packet can be
authorized. A materialization can be adequate for a particular target. Those
are not the same state. A packet may be admitted for drafting but inadequate
for verification. A source may be known but outside the worker's authority. A
summary may be useful for orientation but too lossy for citation.

Certificates keep the units honest. They record the source bindings,
derivation links, transaction references, artifact references, omissions, loss
contract, authority ceiling, permitted uses, validity window, verification
state, revocation state, residual risks, and non-claims. An exact excerpt can
support quotation. A planning summary can help a scheduler choose work. A
redacted execution packet can be shown to a worker without leaking source
material. A reader paraphrase can help a human understand the idea. Each may
be useful, but each is useful under a different contract.

## What The Model Actually Receives

The easiest way to understand the ABI is to picture the packet handed to a
worker model.

In an ungoverned system, the packet may be a block of relevant text. It might
contain a source excerpt, a summary, a user preference, an instruction, an old
memory, and a policy note, all flattened into one prompt. The model can still
produce a fluent answer. The stack cannot easily tell which part was allowed
to support the answer.

With the ABI, the packet is a small bundle of typed cells. One cell may be an
exact excerpt with a source binding and quotation permission. Another may be a
lossy planning summary with orientation-only permission. A third may be a
redacted instruction with a narrow authority ceiling. A fourth may be a
contradiction pair that warns the worker that two sources disagree. A fifth
may be a reader paraphrase that is useful for explanation but not for proof,
citation, or tool authorization.

The worker receives text, but the text arrives with role and scope. The packet
says what can be quoted, what can guide drafting, what can be used only for
planning, what is stale, what is redacted, what expires, what is adequate for
the current task, and what fault remains. If a mandatory source is missing,
the ABI should return a miss. If the lease expired, it should return a stale
context fault. If the only available representation is a lossy summary and
the task requires citation, the packet is inadequate rather than merely less
convenient.

This is the memory equivalent of a type error. A type error is not a failure
of intelligence. It is the system refusing to use an object in a role it was
not allowed to fill. The Virtual Context ABI gives context the same kind of
resistance. It prevents a useful paragraph from silently becoming a source,
an instruction, a proof input, or a permission slip.

This example is still a contract description. It does not claim that the
repository has a deployed resolver, a truthful certificate generator, a
model-facing context runtime, or an open-domain summary-fidelity evaluator.

## Interfaces

Planning requests context by task, address, authority, representation need,
and adequacy target. VCM resolves and compiles it. Spinoza-style claim ledgers
consume claim and evidence cells. Execution receives only representations
whose certificates allow the action and authority context. Artifact graphs
store certificate references when a derived representation becomes part of
durable work.

The minimum context ABI record includes request identity, task identity,
semantic address, version, mount, snapshot, representation contract, authority
ceiling, admission state, adequacy state, fault state, materialization
reference, consumer policy, support boundary, lease expiry, replay boundary,
source references, residuals, audit references, support-state effect, and
non-claims.

The minimum semantic page certificate includes page identity, cell type, source
bindings, derivation links, transaction references, artifact references,
representation kind, loss contract, omissions, authority ceiling, permitted
uses, validity window, verification state, revocation state, residual risks,
and non-claims.

Those fields keep context from silently changing role. A planning summary does
not become a citation because it appears in a claim discussion. A reader
paraphrase does not become proof support because it reads more cleanly than
the source. An exact excerpt does not become executable permission because it
contains an instruction.

## Invariants

Addresses and versions must be stable. A claim should not depend on "whatever
retrieval returned at the time." It should depend on a context reference that
can be reconstructed.

Admission and adequacy must remain distinct. A packet that is safe to show a
worker may still be inadequate for the claim being checked. A packet that is
adequate for orientation may be inadequate for proof, citation, release, or
self-improvement.

Authority labels survive summarization. Loss contracts are explicit. Derived
cells point back to source bindings and to the transaction or artifact record
that created the representation when that record exists. A summary cannot
become more citable, more executable, more secret-cleared, or more verified
than the source and certificate allow.

The current Lean hooks are narrow but useful. They prove finite-record
properties: a resolved context reference must have a matching address,
version, and snapshot binding; a mandatory miss must produce a typed fault; a
valid derived cell must carry source bindings plus loss/use declarations; and
a summary respecting source authority cannot raise its authority ceiling above
the source cells. The VCM resolver/certificate probe adds deterministic
fixture-pair checks for resolver-style materialization and certificate
boundaries. These artifacts do not prove a real resolver, context compiler,
open-domain summary fidelity, or certificate truthfulness.

## Failure Modes

Flat transcript memory loses addresses, versions, leases, authority labels,
and support boundaries. The model remembers text but not the contract around
the text.

Stale context makes an old correct answer look current. Without a snapshot and
lease boundary, the system may reuse a packet after its assumptions have
expired.

Summary overconfidence makes a fluent derived cell sound complete. Provenance
loss keeps the gist but drops the source binding needed to audit, quote,
challenge, or revise it. Authority escalation via compression turns a source
with a narrow authority label into a harmless-looking note and then uses it
where the source itself would have been denied.

Certificate laundering is subtler. The cell has a certificate, so downstream
layers stop asking whether the certificate is current, truthful, and adequate
for the new use. The certificate should make review easier. It should not make
review optional.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a public record surface that can
represent the static ABI and reject a few dishonest collapses.

The repository already has this narrow surface. `schemas/context_abi_record`
and `schemas/semantic_page_certificate` define the public record shapes.
`AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI` covers finite snapshot-binding and
mandatory-miss fault predicates. `AsiStackProofs.ContextCertificates` covers
derived-cell field presence and authority non-escalation. The context
admission/adequacy harness checks synthetic context ABI records, context
packets, semantic page certificates, context transactions, and adequacy
records.

That is meaningful evidence for record discipline. It is not evidence for a
deployed VCM service. It does not prove semantic-address resolver correctness,
summary fidelity, contradiction-rate improvement, distractor resistance,
runtime memory-store behavior, or model-facing context quality.

The next practical slice is now present as the VCM resolver/certificate probe.
It admits a valid resolver materialization receipt and a mandatory-miss fault,
then rejects address, version, and snapshot mismatches; denied mount policies;
expired lease reuse; certificate source-binding errors; authority escalation;
truthfulness overclaiming; and summary-fidelity omission. This is useful record
discipline, not evidence for a deployed resolver, memory store, context
compiler, open-domain summary fidelity, certificate truthfulness, transaction
isolation, deletion enforcement, model-facing context quality, VCM-Bench
performance, leak prevention, or support-state promotion.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature Virtual Context ABI is a memory operating interface for
long-horizon AI work. It is not just a larger retrieval system. It is the
stable syscall layer that lets planners, compilers, workers, verifiers, claim
ledgers, auditors, reader editions, and later AI agents request and receive
context with stable identities and permissioned representations.

With that interface, a model never receives anonymous "relevant context." It
receives a materialization receipt and typed cells. The receipt says what was
requested, which address and version resolved, what representation was built,
which authority labels survived, what was omitted, which uses are permitted,
when the lease expires, which certificate applies, whether the packet is only
admitted or also adequate for the target claim, and which fault or residual
remains.

The endpoint is a governed context fabric: exact source excerpts, summaries,
redactions, planning briefs, claim cells, contradiction pairs, reader
paraphrases, and execution packets can all be materialized from shared sources
without pretending they have the same authority or evidential role.

## Summary

The Virtual Context ABI gives memory a calling convention. It turns context
from anonymous relevant text into typed, leased, certified packets with stable
addresses, versions, authority labels, adequacy states, permitted uses, and
fault paths.

The chapter's practical lesson is that model-visible context should not erase
the contract around the material it contains. A source excerpt, planning
summary, redacted instruction, contradiction pair, and reader paraphrase can
all help the system, but they cannot honestly do the same job. The ABI keeps
those roles separate before downstream reasoning, execution, or publication
depends on them.

## Handoff

The static context interface is the local boundary. The next layer is dynamic
context behavior: which events are committed, which snapshot is visible, which
mount is readable, which branch was used, which derivatives were created, how
taint propagates, and how deletion or declassification obligations close.

That is the job of **Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint**.
